data.title: Osterweis Capital Management Q2 2026 Equity Outlook
data.description:
For the better part of two decades, software companies and information services firms have been rightfully viewed as the archetypal quality compounders. Once built, these businesses have been fortresses protected from competitive encroachment, resulting in significant pricing power and offering extraordinary incremental margins with returns on invested capital (ROIC) that only improve with scale. Furthermore, these businesses have taken share from the analog world, offering a seemingly endless runway for growth. As a result, software and information services companies have been accorded premium multiples by the market.

That playbook is now being stress-tested. The arrival of increasingly capable AI agents — from Anthropic’s Cowork and OpenAI’s Codex to OpenClaw — has prompted a broad selloff across software and information services stocks. In fact, the S&P 500 Software & Services Index, encompassing 140 companies, has fallen almost 25% year-to-date. The market is asking a deceptively simple question: If AI can replicate business logic, crawl public data, and compose user interfaces at near-zero marginal cost, what is the terminal value of a traditional SaaS subscription?

We believe the answer is nuanced. Not all moats are eroding equally, and the nature of competitive advantage is shifting in the AI era. Businesses that will compound value over the next decade are those that are investing aggressively today to build the moats of the future. Below, we share our evolving views.

Technology Companies: Capital Light to Capital Intensive

Gone are the days of exploding free cash flow and expanding margins for dominant technology companies known as hyperscalers. Long celebrated by investors for their capital efficiency, these technology behemoths are now some of the most capital-intensive businesses in the world, as they seek to dominate AI technology by building out the infrastructure to support large language models (LLMs) and the associated agents used by consumers and enterprises.

In fact, the five largest hyperscalers are projected to deploy over $700 billion in aggregate capital expenditure this year, an increase of over 60% from 2025. Most of them are expected to see a significant decline in free cash flow, which we believe will be temporary. In addition, a handful are taking on significant debt to finance their AI infrastructure buildout, and we are avoiding these companies. Newcomers like OpenAI and Anthropic are capital-intensive from the onset, burning cash at an unprecedented pace to build durable moats and network effects as well as grab a slice of the market from the formidable incumbents.

As investors increasingly question the ROIC of such massive cash outlays, there are a few useful precedents to look at. In 2015-2016, Amazon was criticized for spending aggressively on cloud infrastructure at the expense of near-term profitability. Amazon Web Services (AWS) ultimately generated substantial free cash flow, became Amazon’s most valuable and profitable business, and continues to lead in the AI era. Furthermore, Amazon also undertook other major investment cycles in the past to build out its now dominant retail and advertising businesses. Another precedent we would point to is Walmart, which went on a multi-year investment cycle to build out its ecommerce business and fortify its brick-and-mortar retail operations, resulting in record results over the past several years.

Today, the hyperscalers are making a similar wager that massive upfront capital deployment will yield durable competitive advantages in AI infrastructure that cannot be easily replicated. We are beginning to see some evidence of this: Google Cloud’s operating income grew a whopping 127% in the past year, and its operating margin expanded from 14% to 24%, suggesting these investments are yielding great returns for Google. Similarly, Amazon’s AWS division saw revenue growth of 24% in the most recent quarter — the fastest growth in 13 quarters — while operating margin expanded to 35%.

The Asset-Light Software Model is Under Pressure

If high capital intensity is the story for large technology companies providing the AI infrastructure of the future, the story for software companies is one of gradual moat erosion. The traditional software business model — high gross margins, recurring subscription revenue, and high switching costs — faces a two-pronged challenge.

First, future growth is less certain due to AI disruption, with terminal values increasingly in question. AI agents can now replicate interfaces, encode business logic, and orchestrate workflows across tools in ways that diminish the value of a monolithic software bundle. Classic software moats are weakening. Learned interfaces — the keyboard shortcuts and specialized navigation that kept users locked into various software platforms — dissolve when the interface is simply a natural language conversation that novices can use. Business logic that took years of domain-expert engineering to encode in software can now be written in weeks. Public data access of large databases, once a genuine competitive advantage built on armies of custom document parsers, becomes trivial when the foundation model itself can evaluate and parse these databases in seconds.

Second, ROIC may start to erode as companies lose pricing power. AI has dramatically lowered barriers to entry for new software companies. Thousands of AI-native startups have emerged and received a record $150 billion in funding in 2025. They are tackling problems in different verticals and offering consumption-based pricing at steep discounts to existing subscriptions, eroding the revenue base for traditional software companies. While no clear winner has emerged, it is evident that the premium multiples investors once awarded to traditional software companies for pricing power and 90%+ retention rates can no longer be sustained.

But not all software moats are created equal, and we think it is a mistake to paint the entire sector with a single brush. A useful analogy is the media industry in the early 2000s. The advent of the internet eroded one of the most important moats that legacy media companies enjoyed: distribution. Many incumbents were disrupted. But those that owned truly proprietary content — or pivoted towards it, as HBO (part of Warner Bros.) and a handful of others eventually did — survived and, in some cases, thrived. A new class of internet-native winners like Netflix and Spotify emerged, distributing their content online directly to consumers. We see a similar bifurcation playing out today in software. The transition period for some will be long and painful, but we believe certain existing players will emerge stronger on the other side.

Reassessing Moats in the Age of AI

To understand shifting moats in the age of AI, it might be helpful to refer to Hamilton Helmer’s “7 Powers” framework, which is widely cited in business analysis. Helmer identified seven aspects of moats, namely Scale Economies, Network Economies, Process Power, Counter-Positioning, Cornered Resources, Switching Costs, and Branding. Some of these deserve more attention than others for the purpose of this analysis.

Cornered Resources have become increasingly valuable. Compute capacity and talent represent the defining cornered resource in this era. Amazon, Alphabet, and Microsoft are investing aggressively to corner available energy, industrial capacity, and semiconductor supply and doling out generous packages to recruit scarce AI talent.

Scale Economies remain powerful and are increasingly important for spreading out the costs of massive AI investments. Hyperscalers are able to amortize their infrastructure costs across their vast customer bases. The economics of training and deploying frontier AI models inherently favor scale — a dynamic that benefits foundational LLM companies like OpenAI with massive user bases — and will create barriers for smaller entrants.

Counter-Positioning is a highly relevant theme in the AI era. Companies like OpenAI and Anthropic and a swarm of AI-native startups have reinvented business models and scaled up at unprecedented speeds. Software is changing from subscription to consumption-based models. This challenges the well-entrenched incumbents, leaving them with a classic dilemma: engage in creative destruction and thus cannibalize the current revenue base, or try to protect the current business and extract maximum cash flows in the face of a secular threat.

Switching Costs warrant a completely new lens for analyzing certain industries. Software companies that have long prided themselves on the stickiness of their workflows are now seeing those workflows increasingly challenged by AI-native startups. When AI agents can rewrite and migrate legacy databases and workflows in days rather than months, the friction of switching diminishes materially.

Portfolio Positioning: Where We See Durable Quality Growth

The shifts in moats discussed above have real implications on how we construct our portfolio, which is focused on quality growth. We are increasingly distinguishing between businesses whose moats are unaffected or reinforced by the AI transition, and those whose moats are threatened by it.

We are overweight the industrial economy, reflecting our view that physical-world moats built through sustained capital investment are becoming more, not less valuable. For companies in the aerospace and rail industries, decades of accumulated capital, engineering expertise, and regulatory requirements have created barriers that AI cannot easily overcome. These businesses benefited tremendously from scale economies and cornered resources that have been built up over generations.

We are also overweight health care. In pharmaceuticals, patents remain the ultimate cornered resource as leading pharmaceutical companies derive their competitive position from molecules that cannot be replicated until patents expire.

Within technology, we favor the infrastructure layer over the application layer. Although current free cash flow challenges warrant close attention, we believe that the ongoing investment cycle from hyperscalers will ultimately translate to accelerating revenue growth and expanding margins, creating widened scale advantages and generating long-term value. We also remain constructive on semiconductor leaders, particularly those with process power and cornered resources in advanced chip design and manufacturing – the kind of moat that takes decades to build and cannot be replicated through software alone.

In software and information services, we are more selective. We favor companies with truly proprietary data assets, regulatory lock-in, and deeply embedded, critical workflows. We are cautious on companies built around public data curation, workflow automation, or business logic that AI agents can increasingly replicate.

Final Thoughts

We continue to apply our quality growth framework to managing portfolios, as we believe owning high quality businesses with strong incremental returns on capital that can compound free cash flow over time should translate to outperformance in the long run.

What is evolving is our definition of a durable competitive advantage, which we have always believed is an essential element of a quality company. The asset-light, high-ROIC model that defined software investing for a generation is not dead, but it is no longer sufficient. In the age of AI, investors must evaluate not just whether a business generates strong returns today, but whether its competitive moat is widening or narrowing, and whether its position is reinforced or undermined by the broader shift toward capital intensity.

To evaluate the structural durability of a company’s competitive advantages, we now seek moats rooted in physical assets, proprietary data, network effects, regulatory lock-in, and large scale capital investment. We are willing to accept temporary declines in free cash flow when companies are investing to build or reinforce their moats, as we believe that free cash flow should eventually improve and grow as their structural advantages compound.

As always, we thank you for your continued confidence in our management.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Albemarle: Strategic Asset In Energy Security (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

My view that short-term pain would keep Albemarle (ALB) on hold turned into a far longer correction cycle for lithium. At the time (4Q23), I believed LCE (lithium carbonate equivalent) prices had a floor of around $16/kg and would rebound to $20/kg in 2H24. Prices continued to bottom through August 2025, when Chinese mines began to close, and demand from EVs and utility-sized batteries increased, starting a price normalization cycle. ALB and the sector reacted instantly, and now the question is whether there is upside or if the LCE price will decline again. In my view, the Ukraine invasion and the Iran war have materially altered the world's energy matrix, and nations will increasingly seek renewables (wind/solar) in conjunction with battery storage to meet energy demands made more pressing by AI/data-centric needs. Not to mention the consumer and transport cost increase from higher oil prices, which makes EV replacement palatable.

ALB Operating Model

ALB does not disclose LCE volumes, and capacity data is further complicated by JVs in Australia. Nonetheless, I have built a simplified model that captures the LCE price increase. In the table below, I am using consensus volume/capacity expansion, which is modest at under 7% through 2028. I then plugged in my LCE price expectations of $18/kg for 2026, which yields a revenue estimate. The consensus has a wide dispersion in LCE prices, from $10/kg to $20/kg, as many analysts (including me) were burned during the last down cycle or correction.

On the cost side, the Chile assets have a heavy sales tax of up to 40% when LCE is over $10/kg and eat into margins as scale gains are diluted; nonetheless, EBITDA margins should break 50% after several years of forced productivity measures, including the shutdown of a hydroxide process plant in Australia this year. Note that total capacity/volumes are over 100 tons higher than in the peak 2022/2023 period, helping drive higher EBITDA in absolute terms despite a lower average LCE price.

The specialty chemical business is expected to experience a decline in demand, but it has good margins and should contribute under 12% to consolidated EBITDA.

Since ALB does not consolidate its Australian assets, in which it holds a 50% stake, the EBITDA line is reduced, and the net income from these JVs is recorded below the EBIT line as equity income. In addition, during the LCE price collapse, ALB took several write-downs and restructuring charges that hurt GAAP net income, which are largely non-cash items. An adjusted net income is also calculated.

ALB Cash Flow

ALB proactively responded to the LCE price decline by cutting capex by a third, shuttering less cost-effective plants, selling assets, and raising over $2.2 bn in preferred shares. This allowed the company to reduce debt and maintain a good balance sheet. With the LCE's modest price increase to the $20/kg level, the company should be able to generate over $1.5bn in FCF, which could be used to increase dividends. Note that about half the 2025 dividend is mandatory and paid only to the preferred shares.

Valuation

While the operating side of the ALB business case is relatively straightforward, with LCE prices driving earnings and cash flow, valuation is complicated by the company's JVs. Most analyses utilize an adjusted EV/EBITDA multiple to reach a price target, which entails adding the JV's net income to EBITDA. I view this as inaccurate and mixing methodologies and prefer to value the shares on a PE basis that more accurately captures ALB's debt costs and taxes, as well as core lithium earnings.

The current consensus price tag is $189, with a target PE of 18.7x. Not that the PEG ratio is not usable for 2026, given that adjusted earnings are coming from losses. Given that this is a cyclical commodity company, I prefer to value the stock at a fixed PE of 15x, which implies that shares already reflect an average LCE price of $18/kg in 2026.

However, given the shift in global energy markets, where supply and price shocks can wreak havoc on many economies, there will most likely be higher demand for renewable energy (wind/solar) that operates best with utility-scale storage/batteries that require lithium. As I recently wrote in a Cameco (CCJ) report, uranium is now more strategic than ever; I believe lithium has moved into this category as well, and low-cost producers have become strategic assets with valuation premiums.

Peer Comps

As can be seen in the table below, the sector has monster revenue and EPS growth in 2026 as LCE prices work their magic. Many companies reported losses in 2025, and thus, the comparison base is highly distorted. Consensus price targets have been breached, and further LCE price gains or resilience are needed to support upgrades. In my view, Sigma Lithium (SGML) still has significant execution risk to achieve consensus growth. While Sociedad Química y Minera de Chile (SQM) may also benefit from the rise in fertilizer prices. The recent CATL report was highly positive, suggesting that battery makers are experiencing increased demand.

Risk

The main risk to ALB is lithium prices and, more specifically, a resumption of mine expansion and potential overcapacity that could lead to lower prices. Environmental and judgment risk may reemerge, especially in Chile and new sites in Argentina, which can threaten production and costs.

Conclusion

I upgraded ALB to buy from the hold. I am late to upgrade; the LCE price recovery that began in August 2025 seemed temporary and dangerous, given that it was driven by mine closures. Today, the fundamental drivers of lithium have changed; it's now a highly strategic molecule for energy security, and ALB is one of the largest and lowest-cost producers, poised to see significant earnings and cash flow gains over the next three years.

data.title: Why Amazon Is Not The AI Chip Provider Broadcom Is
data.description:
Despite being the #1 hyperscaler during the current AI boom, Amazon (AMZN) has arguably been an under-performing stock over the past 5-years (see chart below). That lack of performance has been frustrating and motivated me to write the highly read Seeking Alpha article: "Amazon: It's Time To Spin-off AWS". That being the case, I was very pleased to see Amazon CEO Andy Jassy finally give more color on the company's $200 billion cap-ex plan in his 2025 Letter To Shareholders released on Thursday. It was the most bullish report I can remember since Jassy took over the CEO job from Jeff Bezos. In fact, the AI chips narrative was so bullish that Barron's analyst Adam Clark wrote an article putting Amazon's AI chip business in the same class as Broadcom (AVGO) - see "Amazon Stock Posts Biggest Gain in More Than 5 Months. Why CEO Letter Has the Market Excited." While I am certainly happy that Amazon's AI chip business is booming, today I will explain why it is no Broadcom.

As you can see from the 5-year total returns chart above, Amazon stock has not only significantly lagged its hyperscaler peers, but its returns were doubled by the S&P500 (as represented by the (VOO) ETF) and were clearly not even been in the same league as Broadcom. Let's take a look to see if assertions made in the Barron's article are believable.

Investment Thesis

Before I get into the nuts-n-bolts of Jassy's shareholder letter, one of the big picture takeaways from Amazon's letter to shareholders is more evidence of what I have been saying in Seeking Alpha commentary over the past few years: the AI inference market is likely to become larger than the training market. That's because there are only a handful of companies that are focused on training frontier LLM's: Google (GOOG), Anthropic (ANTHRO), OpenAI (OPENAI), META (META), Deep Seek, and Mistral AI. However, practically every large company on planet Earth will likely need to use AI inference to optimize its business. What this means, and as companies like Broadcom and Amazon have clearly demonstrated, there is a huge market for more cost-efficient, less power hungry AI compute engine solutions for optimized inference algorithms than what can be achieved using leading AI chipmaker NVidia's (NVDA) GPUs.

Amazon Letter To Shareholders: Highlights

Amazon CEO Jassy's 2025 Letter To Shareholders was just what the market wanted and needed to hear after its massive $200 billion cap-ex plan was initially communicated, took investors by surprise, and drove the stock down. What took the company so long to add the additional color and rationale behind the surprisingly large cap-ex announcement is still somewhat of a mystery to me. In fact, I even considered selling a bit more of my Amazon stock to add shares of better performing positions in Google and Broadcom and would have been extremely irritated if I had actually sold some shares only to see the Letter to Shareholders released (the stock jumped $12.40/share yesterday, or 5.6%).

In my opinion, the pop in Amazon shares was primarily because of two of Jassy's six points (numbers 4 & 5):

4/ Our chips business is on fire, changes the economics for AWS, and will be much larger than most think.

In the fourth bullet, and after assuring AWS users of the strong partnership with NVIDIA, Jassy correctly pointed out that cloud consumers are looking for better price-performance solutions. He then noted the success of Graviton (an x86-based solution) and Trainium-3, which started shipping this year and has 30-40% more price/performance than Trainium-2, and which is "nearly fully-subscribed". Capacity for Trainium-4, which is not yet available, already has a "significant chunk reserved". In Jassy's words, "Demand for Trainium is booming", and that's great news for AWS Bedrock, because the inference platform primarily runs on Trainium chips.

Jassy said having a home-grown AI chip significantly improves AWS economics:

At scale, we expect Trainium will save us tens of billions of capex dollars per year, and provide several hundred basis points of operating margin advantage versus relying on others’ chips for inference.

Jassy also said that Amazon's AI chip-business is now at a $20+ billion annual run-rate and growing at triple digits while selling only to AWS's EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud) business:

The EC2 architecture shown above is both interesting and attractive: each EC2 instance is a virtual server that runs in the AWS Cloud. When an EC2 instance is launched, the user can specify the instance type by determining the hardware available for the chosen EC2 instance. That's because for each EC2 instance type, the customer can configure the compute, memory, network, and storage resources that will be available to it (see graphic above). The more resources a customer uses, the higher the cost. Such a flexible architecture enables AWS customers to configure EC2 instances in order to optimize price-performance points for their specific inference algorithm workflow.

Jassy goes on to claim that, if Amazon's AI chips were a stand-alone business and sold chips to AWS and third parties, the annual run-rate would be ~$50 billion. That statement alone seems to advance my argument that Amazon should spin-off AWS (including the chip business), but that's another debate for another time. Meantime, Jassy said:

There’s so much demand for our chips that it’s quite possible we’ll sell racks of them to third parties in the future.

I guess we'll have to wait-n-see on the third-party sale. My take is that if Marvell (MRVL) - Amazon's AI chips partner - can reserve enough TMSC capacity over-and-above AWS' needs, we could well see Amazon sell to third-parties.

The second market-moving point was the commentary around point number six:

6/ We have customer commitments that make our capex investments predictable.

Here, Jassy said Amazon is not investing $200 billion in cap-ex on a "hunch" and Cites OpenAI's $100 billion commitment as exhibit #1 while hinting at more significant unannounced agreements that are currently "deep in process". More important in my opinion was Jassy's assertion:

Of the AWS capex we expect to spend in 2026, much of which will be monetized in 2027-2028, we already have customer commitments for a substantial portion of it.

So, all of this was great and much-needed clarity on Amazon's $200 billion cap-ex plan - as they say, better late then never. Especially that much of the spending will be monetized in 2027-28.

All of this is great news for Amazon shareholders. But here's why Amazon isn't in the same class as Broadcom when it comes to AI chips.

The Broadcom Advantage

First of all, and even as Jassy's letter points out, Amazon is not selling its AI chips to third parties, and given the strong internal demand and likely supply constraints, it's unclear if and when Amazon will be able to do so. As a result, all Amazon's AI chips are consumed in-house (in AWS) and, that being the case, they do not have a direct high-margin return to shareholders other than the indirect growth and profits of AWS.

That is a big difference as compared to Broadcom, which focuses on selling directly to a handful of high-volume hyperscaler customers like Google, META, Anthropic, OpenAI, and Byte Dance with multi-year XPU supply agreements. These same hyperscalers may be hesitant to buy chips from a fellow hyperscaler like Amazon.

To my knowledge Broadcom does not use any of its chip designs in house (other than for testing, verification, and validation purposes) and sell their XPUs directly to its hyperscaler customers. What this means is that Broadcom shareholders - unlike Amazon shareholders - have a very direct and clear line-of-sight into high-margin returns from the company's XPU sales. Exhibit #1 is the recent stock-moving news which I am sure you are all aware of: the low-key Broadcom filing that reported:

Broadcom Inc. (“Broadcom”) and Google LLC (“Google”) have entered into a Long Term Agreement for Broadcom to develop and supply custom Tensor Processing Units (“TPUs”) for Google’s future generations of TPUs and a Supply Assurance Agreement for Broadcom to supply networking and other components to be used in Google’s next-generation AI racks through up to 2031.

In my opinion, that just extended Broadcom shareholders' clear line-of-sight into XPU and networking chips profits I was just speaking about until 2031. Indeed, as Seeking Alpha reported, analyst Atif Malik of Citi said there is the possibility of “upside” to Broadcom's AI revenue target of $100B to $130B. Malik reiterated his Buy rating and has $475 price target on Broadcom.

Secondly, and as pointed out in the previously referenced Seeking Alpha article recommending Amazon spin-off AWS, Amazon shareholders are not getting the direct benefit of its booming AI chip's business because the margin of AWS is being dragged down by the poor margins of Amazon's retail business. For example, in FY2025:

  • AWS Margin: 35%
  • North America & International Margin: 5.8%
  • Consolidated Operating Margin: 11.2%
  • Free-cash-flow Margin: $11.2 billion FCF/$716.9 billion revenue = 1.6%.

So, sure, it's great that Amazon is doing so well with its AI chips - boosting AWS efficiency and margin - but note that Broadcom's margins in FY2025 were as follows:

  • Gross Margin: 67.8%
  • Operating Margin: 39.9%
  • Free-cash-flow Margin: $26.9 billion FCF/$63.887 billion revenue = 42%.

The point here being that AWS margin of 35% is several percentage points below Broadcom's consolidated free-cash-flow margin of 42% while Amazon's consolidated free-cash-flow margin of 1.6% clearly isn't even in the same league as Broadcom's 42%. And, I'd note, Broadcom's FY25 FCF margin is actually on the low-end of its historical prints given that the company is still digesting the debt taken on from its relatively recent and large VMWare acquisition.

Clearly, the free-cash-flow trajectories of the two companies have been going in opposite directions:

As you can see from the graphic, Amazon's Q4 FY25 free-cash-flow of $11.2 billion was -71% yoy. Meantime, Broadcom's Q1 FY26 free-cash-flow of $8.0 billion was +33% yoy while full-year FY2025 FCF generation was +38.6% and more than double the FCF Amazon generated on it massively larger revenue base:

Thirdly, Broadcom's AI XPU compute engines are just part of the total package it offers by way of what I have termed its "AI Trifecta" lineup: XPU compute engines, high-speed networking products, and its WMWare world-class virtualization and enterprise software. Yes, Amazon has its homegrown EC2 NIC (Network Interface Card), but it has nothing to compete with Broadcom's leading-edge line of high-speed Tomahawk Ethernet Switches and integrated DSP and SerDes solutions.

So, when Broadcom signs an XPU supply agreement, customers also typically bundle the XPUs with high-speed networking solutions that are optimized for that specific compute engine and their AI data-center architecture. As I have been pointing out, for years Broadcom has said that as AI scales-out and scales-up with thousands (or even 10's of thousands) of networked XPUs (and other compute engines) - and even across data centers, the network is the computer. Indeed, Amazon itself is a buyer of Broadcom's networking chips, although the relationship has been described as "frosty".

In addition, many of Broadcom's AI customers also subscribe to Broadcom's VMWare virtualization software due to the fact that VMWare has signed strategic agreements that enable flexible support for use of NVIDIA's GPUs, AMD's (AMD) GPUs, and - of course - Broadcom's XPUs, all of which can be mixed-n-matched to provide simultaneous support for leading-edge LLM training on NVIDIA GPUs, and optimized inference using Broadcom's XPUs, for example.

Summary & Conclusions

While I appreciate the reporting in Barron's, and with all due respect to the esteemed publication and the analyst, in my opinion it is way too early to believe Amazon's AI chip business is going to be enough to significantly move the needle for AMZN shareholders or that its AI chip business poses a threat and/or is any way on the same level as Broadcom's XPU business. As I have been writing on Seeking Alpha, the 2H of this year is going to be the big break-out moment for Broadcom because it is ramping up multiple new customer XPU designs on TSCM's leading edge 2nm process. Many of these XPU solutions will be housed in Broadcom's leading-edge packaging solutions and will be bundled with Broadcom's networking products. By the end of this year, it will be absolutely clear to investors that Broadcom's AI chips business is not only miles ahead of Amazon's, but that Broadcom is taking market share away from NVIDIA. That said, as a long-suffering Amazon shareholder, I was grateful for CEO Jassy's Letter To Shareholders - its most bullish report in years.

I maintain my BUY rating on Amazon (+15% since my last article) due to AI chip momentum, strong AWS growth, and opportunities across its platform to monetize AI - specifically, in advertising, which could grow 20%+ this year. Consolidated revenue growth should be in a range of 13-15% for both 2026 and 2027 due to AWS accelerating to ~25% growth due to strong AI demand and the $244 billion backlog. The stock could rise to the $300 area based on a 30x multiple on a FY27 GAAP earnings estimate of $10/share.

On Broadcom, I maintain my STRONG BUY rating in light of the stock's high-margin revenue growth, back-end loaded 2H XPU shipments, and the companies demonstrated strong dividend growth and share buyback plans. Broadcom should generate $50 billion in free-cash-flow this year (an estimated $10.30/share) and $70 billion of FCF in FY27. According to TipRanks, the average price target among the 31 analysts covering Broadcom is $464. At pixel time, that is 24% above Broadcom's current price ($374.12) but $25 below the 4-start rated and (typically conservative) Morningstar fair-value estimate of $500:

Risks to both companies include high market volatility due to the continually fallout from the on-going war with Iran and its impact on market sentiment, high concentration of semiconductor fabrication capacity in Taiwan (i.e. TSMC), and a potential slackening in the AI data-center boom. High oil and thus gasoline prices will potentially have a significantly more negative impact on Amazon's retail business than it will on either of the two companies' AI chip sales.

I hope my followers took advantage of the recent dip in Broadcom shares to add to their positions. The company has come roaring back - just as I predicted (see Broadcom: What So Many Analysts & Investors Got Wrong - Buy The Dip).

I'll end by noting that Broadcom's superior FCF profile has enabled the company to grow its dividend at a 10% rate over the past few years while also implementing significant share buybacks. A few years back, in a bout of 'returns frustration', I sold a small percentage of my AMZN shares and invested the proceeds into Broadcom. The difference in the 3-year total returns has been stunning and speak for themselves (see chart below). I see no reason why the next three years will be much different. That is, unless CEO Jassy and team take my advice and spin-off AWS.

data.title: Global Partners: Watch Out For The Redemption Of The Preferred Shares
data.description:
Thesis

We last covered the Series B Preferred Shares (GLP.PR.B) from the MLP Global Partners (GLP) a year ago, when we authored an article that showed our expectations for a one year remaining maturity on the securities. With the first call date for GLP.PR.B upon us (in May 2026), we are going to show investors why the securities will get called, thus avoiding any lost cost of opportunity by parking cash here.

The fundamental performance by the operating company is strong

The MLP has done very well in the past year, posting healthy EBITDA and net income figures:

The product margin and gross profit figures have been very constant year over year, with the net income figure slightly lower due to the higher necessary maintenance capex. As an MLP we did expect the company to have 'sticky' financials, but GLP is extremely well run and has a reliable performing business model. We can see that in the segmentation of the revenue generators for the business as well:

While the wholesale business was slightly higher year-on-year, the GDSO business segment saw a slight contraction, while the commercial segment was largely stable.

Robust balance sheet

As an MLP, the company runs leverage:

The leverage ratio as of December 2025 was 3.6x, which is on the high side for non-MLP businesses. In fact GLP had issued preferred shares in the past due to high leverage and the need to keep that in check. At a point the company's leverage was close to 5x (after the pandemic when gas demand plummeted initially), which meant it needed alternative forms of financing in order to avoid hitting bond covenants:

In the world of corporate finance, bond leverage covenants are essentially the "guardrails" that lenders (bondholders) put on a company to ensure it doesn't take on too much debt. Think of it like a bank giving you a mortgage but stating you can’t go out and finance five Ferraris the next day. It’s all about protecting the lender's investment by maintaining the borrower's creditworthiness.

A company which is constrained by leverage ratios will raise additional capital by issuing either preferred or common shares. Both forms of financing sit on the equity side of the balance sheet, thus do not affect leverage ratio calculations.

The first series of preferred equity was called upon its first call date, an occurrence which we covered on the Seeking Alpha platform:

In January 2024, we issued $450.0 million of senior unsecured notes and redeemed the $69.0 million of Series A Preferred Units, to enhance our liquidity and optimize our balance sheet. (Company SEC filing)

In order to make an informed decision regarding the likelihood of the company calling the Series B preferred shares on their first call date (May 15, 2026) we need to look at their current cost of capital and their needs.

The company's debt is trading very well in the market

The MLP has the following bonds currently outstanding:

  • $400M 7.00% senior notes due 2027,
  • $350M 6.875% senior notes due 2029
  • $450M 8.250% senior notes due 2032

We can gauge the market interest in the company by looking at secondary trading levels and spreads for the existing bonds. The 2029 notes for example are trading roughly at par, implying a cost of capital for three year debt of close to 6.9% (before any underwriting fees). The longest dated ones are trading at a premium to par, with an implied yield to maturity of roughly 8%. These are very attractive levels when compared to the 9.5% coupon on the preferred shares.

The secondary market is telling us there is ample appetite for GLP credit, and the company's fundamentals agree with that view. Given the company's leverage ratio is on the lower side historically for this MLP, there is no need to have preferred shares outstanding. The cost of capital equation is very, very simple, with over 3.5% saved by retiring GLP.PR.B and just using shorter term debt. We think that retiring the preferred shares is a no-brainer in the current company format (fundamentals plus debt secondary trading).

The current trading dynamics for the securities are:

  • Price: $25.57
  • Accrued interest: $0.44

The securities are trading close to par when stripping out the accrued interest (preferred shares do not trade like OTC bonds with accrued), but we still consider that a bit too high. We are penciling in a call on the first call date here, thus any cash parked in GLP.PR.B will have a flat rate of return when stripping out the accrued interest.

We are keen to see if the company might consider a new series of preferred shares via an at-the-market program, but the fundamentals do not support such a view (i.e. the company has no need for preferred equity in its current capital structure).

Conclusion

We are holders of the GLP.PR.B securities, and the first call date for them is approaching in May 2026. The company is doing very well fundamentally, exhibiting healthy EBITDA and net income figures. The MLP's bonds are trading with very tight yields on the secondary, pointing towards much cheaper financing options. With a low leverage ratio and strong fundamentals we see the MLP redeeming the securities shortly. The stripped price for GLP.PR.B does not present any arbitrage opportunities, being priced for a redemption.

data.title: International Consolidated Airlines: Hedging Provides Cushion Amid Oil Shock, We Still See Upside
data.description:
It's been some time since our last analysis (August 2025), so we are back to comment on International Consolidated Airlines Group (ICAGY) (BABWF). As seen in Figure 1, the company’s share price has increased by 5% but retraced following the escalation in the Middle East. This is a good momentum to review the IAG investment equity story.

As a reminder, our buy rating was supported by solid execution, a strong balance sheet, and ongoing cost transformation. At that time, lower fuel costs and capacity constraints provide additional support for valuation. Moreover, we recognized the IAG Loyalty platform as an earnings growth engine and, for this reason, we reiterated the buy rating. At that time, the oil price was $60- $65 per barrel; today, it is approximately $100 per barrel. Having said that, we have maintained a buy rating on Deutsche Lufthansa AG, and we still see upside for International Consolidated Airlines Group.

Fig 1

Why are we still Positive?

First, we note that EU airline companies usually hedge their fuel needs. Historically, this has been set between 50% and 80% over a 6-24 month time horizon. Again, this provides a lower margin but adds earnings visibility and reduces P&L volatility. On the contrary, American Airlines and United Airlines apply no hedging strategy; Southwest Airlines was the exception (but decided not to roll over its hedging strategy starting in Q2 2025), and Delta owns a refinery (so it is an indirect hedging strategy). Therefore, this provides support for the EU airlines. In particular, International Consolidated Airlines Group has hedged 62% in 2026 (Fig. 2). Therefore, there are two current upside in owning the company: 1) flat price with higher market share due to an increase in occupancy rate, and 2) same occupancy rate but higher earnings given the fact that US airline companies need to increase their ticket prices (due to higher fuel costs). While we see a limited impact in Q1, this will be evident in the Q2 earnings season.

Fig 2

Here at the Lab, we continue to see uncertainty around forecasts given the Middle East conflict. That said, IAG reported the region with Africa, the Middle East, and South Asia. In 2025, the company carried 6.6 million passengers in the area. This accounted for 5.4% of the total number of passengers and included various areas. Looking at the analysis reported that "British Airways increased flying to Amman, Delhi, Jeddah and Johannesburg, at the same time reducing capacity to Abu Dhabi, Bahrain, Doha and Kuwait. British Airways and Iberia resumed flights to Tel Aviv in October. The other main changes in capacity to the region were driven by Vueling, which increased flying from Barcelona to Istanbul and Morocco, whilst reducing flights from Paris to Morocco." We believe the intense competitive environment in the Emirates was already acting as a headwind to the reallocation of flight capacity to other regions.

The company reported its results before the escalation in the Middle East. But they beat EBIT expectation by 1.5%, and announced a higher share buyback of €1.5bn (6% of market cap). More importantly, during the Q&A analyst call, the company emphasized strong Q1 demand, supported by an early Easter and 30% of summer capacity already booked. Additionally, the company reported positive commentary from US network carriers, with premium leisure and corporate demand resilient in all markets. Again, this is not the first time that airlines have managed a crisis and energy shocks, but this time, IAG's net debt-to-EBITDA is 0.8x (Fig. 3), after negotiating lower interest rates.

Fig 3

Earnings Changes And Valuation

At this stage, it is not easy to provide a 2026 forecast, but given that the company is 62% hedged, it will have to procure the unhedged jet kerosene, resulting in pressure on margins. Additionally, the aviation industry may reduce capacity due to lower travel demand and higher prices. On the upside, demand for IAG's business remains supportive, and the company has limited travel capacity in the Middle East. In the results, IAG widened its leverage target from 1.2-1.5x to 1-1.5x. This is due to higher investment CAPEX. Still, IAG's leverage is supportive, and the company has a solid FCF generation with optionality to reduce its buyback. Here at the Lab, we see several risks related to crack spread evolution, counterparty risk on fuel hedges (if supply becomes more constrained), and flying availability, given that 25%/30% of European jet kerosene comes from the Middle East. This is not our base case scenario. We believe this escalation is ending, and there will be no flight disruptions for the summer. IAG will likely benefit from its LatAm upside, where the operating environment appears relatively stable compared with other regions. The company beats our 2025 estimates both on EBIT, EPS, and net leverage. Considering the current operating environment, we believe the company will report lower numbers in 2026 (vs. its initial Fig. 4 assumption), particularly in the EBIT evolution. However, we see this as a negative momentum as an opportunity to increase/start a new position. Considering an EBIT of $5 billion in 2026 (aligned with 2025), fuel cost moves from €7.4 billion at $60 per barrel at the end of February, compared to an estimated impact of €7 billion at the end of December. Therefore, there is a ~€80 million per $1/bbl. At a price of $100 per barrel, the gross impact is €3 billion, with a net impact after hedging of €1.1 billion. This is the maximum impact, but not our base assumption; as the oil price increases so rapidly, we believe it will return to a range between $70-80 over 2026, so we estimate only $500 million headwinds. This results in a forecasted EBIT of $4.5 billion, and, after adding our D&A, our IAG EBITDA reached €7.4 billion. With net interest expense of €360 million and a 25% tax rate, we see net income of €3.1 billion. With shares outstanding of 4.51 billion, our EPS moved to 68c. Continuing to apply an EV/EBITDA of 4x (with a net debt of €5.9 billion), we see 16% upside from the current price. Compared to our last price, we have lowered the IAG target price to £4.55. 1 ADR is equal to 2 ordinary shares, so this results in a $12.2 price.

Fig 4

Risks

We have long covered IAG risks and already reported downside in our analysis. So, we report a higher oil price environment with limited availability of jet kerosene in the EU. Higher competition in Europe, with competitors moving capacity in the region. This might result in a supply/demand imbalance. Nat Cat events and terrorist actions are still present. This escalation might reduce travel demand for both business and leisure. With lower trips, the IAG loyalty business division might also suffer.

Conclusion

We are aware that we assumed a lower oil price environment (still much higher than the 2025 average) and a normalized travel environment (despite travel disruption in some regions). But we believe the company's hedging and routes are better protected than peers'. So, we reiterate our buy rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Bloom Energy: 115x Earnings Is Not Expensive Enough
data.description:
I initiate coverage on Bloom Energy Corporation (BE) with a buy rating mainly because I believe the company is in the right place at the right time.

As data centers are expanding faster than utilities can deliver new capacity, hyperscalers are looking for the quickest "time to power" solutions. I see two main beneficiaries in this space: GE Vernova (GEV) and Bloom Energy.

Even though I am bullish on both companies, I have less conviction in Bloom Energy due to its margin story and valuation, two topics that I discuss below. However, despite the deterioration in margins and the sky-high P/E multiple, I see a stock that offers a practical solution to a real and growing bottleneck in the AI data center buildout.

With headlines (or should I say, subheadlines) like the one below, I believe the grotesquely expensive P/E multiple of the company has plenty of room to rerate higher.

Company Overview

It's difficult to find a clearer slide than the one below to describe what the company does:

Bloom's core product is called Energy Server, an on-site solid oxide fuel cell (SOFC) system that generates electricity without combustion. As a side note, an Energy Server is made up of many power modules, each nicknamed Bloom Box.

The main advantage of these cells is the speed of deployment and modularity. Therefore, one shouldn't be surprised to see data center and utilities companies among the top buyers. Recent relationships with big names include Oracle (ORCL), Equinix (EQIX), American Electric Power (AEP), and Brookfield.

The company says that it can deploy a model to deliver power in as little as 90 days. In fact, during the Q4 2025 earnings call, management said that they recently delivered a hyperscale AI-factory order in 55 days against a 90-day commitment.

Given that utility and developer expectations appear to be misaligned by roughly 1.5-2 years in some markets, time to power is one of the main moats of the company.

How about GE Vernova (a stock that I also own in my portfolio)?

Well, the main difference with GE Vernova is the fact that gas turbines are combustion machines, while Bloom's solid oxide fuel cell modules make electricity through an electrochemical process, still using natural gas, but without combustion.

So, in theory, they are cleaner than conventional natural gas turbines.

On top of that, the Bloom Box modules are 800-volt DC-ready, which is the standard in AI data centers. In contrast, gas turbines produce AC power, which needs to be converted to DC using transformers (which, by the way, are in short supply in the U.S.).

On the flip side, each of these modules delivers significantly less power than GE Vernova's heavy-duty turbines (34 to 571 MW). However, they are compact and modular, which means that even a smaller data center (think Bitcoin miners turning to AI compute) can use this power system in a relatively short period of time.

Time To Power

In the last year, Bloom Energy has re-rated from a boring, clean distributed power energy company to a mission-critical infrastructure play in the AI data center buildout.

If we zoom out for a second, the EIA projects U.S. power demand to increase from 4,195 billion kWh in 2025 to 4,244 billion kWh in 2026 and 4,381 billion kWh in 2027.

The driver behind this growth seems to be AI. Some sources suggest that data center electricity use could more than quadruple by the end of the decade to as much as 17% of U.S. power supplies. In fact, PJM Interconnection (which covers the biggest data center market in the world) is projecting supply shortages as early as next year.

For this reason, developers are increasingly leaning on onsite power generation as the main solution for the power bottleneck. The survey data below speaks volumes about the change in perception for onsite power generation between mid 2024 and the end of 2025.

So, the backdrop is very favorable for a company like Bloom or GE Vernova. That's why I'm bullish on both, even though they are direct competitors in the data center power space.

Strong Backlog

At the end of last quarter, the company had a total backlog of $20B.

Approximately $6B of that amount comes from current product backlog, which is up roughly 2.5x yoy. The remaining $14B is attributed to service backlog, up 46% yoy.

Interestingly, the latest 10-K provides key details about service backlog:

We view service backlog to consist of revenue attributable to contracted operation and maintenance services associated with past and committed future sales of Energy Server product. It includes future service revenue for installed Energy Servers as well as Energy Servers to be delivered and installed in the future. The terms of the contracted operations and maintenance services range from 5 to 20 years, subject to termination for convenience on an annual basis.

That's a sticky service, if you ask me.

In regard to the mix, the backlog includes half a dozen hyperscalers and neocloud companies. Even though they didn't disclose the names, that number alone tells me that the main AI data center players are in the company's customer list.

Risks

The main risk that I see is revenue moving to the right due to delays in the AI data center buildout, driven by factors that are mainly external to the company.

The last 10-K provides more color into these risks:

Many factors can cause a lag between the time a customer signs a contract and our recognition of product revenue. These factors include the number of Energy Server systems installed per site, local permitting and utility requirements, environmental, health and safety requirements, weather, and customer facility construction schedules. Many of these factors are unpredictable and their resolution is often outside of our or our customers' control. Customers may also ask us to delay installation for reasons unrelated to the foregoing, including operational considerations or delays in their financing arrangements.

I find the latter statement above quite interesting, given the fact that no other than Oracle is one of Bloom's customers:

From a company-wide perspective, I am not particularly excited about the decline in gross margins. In the last quarter, non-GAAP gross margin fell to 31.9% from 39.3%. Also, during the same quarter, product gross margin fell to 36.6% from 46.2%, and installation gross margin moved to -10.7% from +5.5%.

As for the rationale, Q4 installation revenue rose to $67.3 million from $36.1 million, but installation cost increased even faster to $74.5 million from $34.1 million. To me, that suggests the quarter included a heavier mix of lower-margin or harder to execute site work. For the deterioration in product margin in Q4, management didn't provide more color beyond a project mix variability.

However, on a full-year 2025 basis, the company did mention three specific drags on product gross profit. I list them below:

  1. $21.9 million of inventory reserve and other impairments tied to electrolyzer assets.
  2. $12.7 million impairment related to construction-in-progress and warehousing/manufacturing infrastructure
  3. $15.9 million reduction to product revenue tied to share-based consideration payable to a key hyperscaler customer.

Overall, I would keep a close eye on margins in Q1 2026 to see if the margin deterioration persists.

Valuation

This is anything but a cheap value stock. On a forward P/E basis, investors are paying today 115x for next year's earnings. In comparison, GE Vernova's 68x forward P/E multiple looks cheap.

Without a doubt, there is a lot of retail participation, and Bloom Energy is starting to feel like a crowded trade. That's why my rating is a buy and not a strong buy, as I reserve the latter for GE Vernova.

From a revenue growth perspective, the Street expects an acceleration through the December 2026 quarter.

Beyond that, it seems that growth is likely to slow down, which is not particularly exciting, given the 115x forward multiple.

Looking Ahead

I am cautiously bullish on Bloom Energy as I believe the stock still has room to rerate higher as long as the power bottleneck in the AI data center buildout persists.

I like the idea behind the Bloom Box, as it's a clean, modular power unit that fits the needs of smaller data centers that cannot justify the investment (or the waiting times) for a conventional gas turbine.

I also like the visibility that the $20B backlog brings, even though the conversion into revenue largely depends on factors outside of the company's reach.

For 2026, Bloom guided revenue in the range of $3.1 billion-$3.3 billion, 32% non-GAAP gross margin, and $425 million-$475 million of non-GAAP operating income. Given the deterioration in margins last year, I would keep a close eye on the bottom line of the company in the upcoming earnings release.

Despite the poor margin story and the outrageous P/E multiple, I own the stock. That said, my favourite AI data center power generation play remains GE Vernova.

data.title: McGraw-Hill: The EdTech Sleeping Giant
data.description:
McGraw-Hill, Inc (MH) is historically known as textbooks publisher for schools and universities. However, the numbers reveal that the company is on track to be a major EdTech player driven by its significant shift from being a one-to-one student sales model to B2B through programs such as inclusive access and Sharpen Advantage platform with recurring revenues of 82% and gross margins exceeding 85%. As a result, I rate MH as a strong buy with a price target of $23, regardless of the debt burden and lawsuits, which I think is short-term noise.

The AI & Technology As a Catalyst

The company has effectively utilized AI either to increase its profits or to add value to students. Its AI reader allows the students to request alternative explanations, add notes, or take personalized quizzes. This tool achieved remarkable success with 27 million interactions since its launch. Furthermore, MH uses the AI to generate and update the educational content through its internal scribe platform, reducing the cost of content development and consequently increasing profit margins.

Additionally, the management transformed its Sharpen Advantage platform into an enterprise solution, sold to institutions and universities, as it connects administrators with professors and students in a single platform, which enhances teamwork and deepens the company's penetration within universities.

Financials And Outlook

Historically, the third quarter ending last December is considered quiet compared to the back-to-school quarter. However, it achieved exceptional results.

Higher Education

The higher education segment was the star of Q3 as it generated revenues of $225.4 million with an increase of 24% YoY. Importantly, the recurring revenues grew by 33.5%, while the total digital revenues increased by 24.8%.

This growth was primarily driven by the Inclusive Access program, which currently represents 60% of higher education revenues. After digitalizing course materials, it became available for all students, and the course cost is included in university fees, which eliminated the piracy and used book market. So the company now guarantees revenue from every student enrolled in the course each semester.

Additionally, the digital content is continuously updated through Evergreen platform; therefore, the company no longer needs to convince professors to update the content and simultaneously saves the costs of printing and distributing. As a result, the market share of McGraw-Hill reached 30% of the higher education sector in the US, according to MDI.

The K-12 segment

Revenues of the Basic Education sector declined by 14.6% to record $128.2 million as the K-12 market is smaller than higher education due to its cyclical nature, as it is going through adoption cycle every 5 or 8 years during which the state allocates a significant budget to update the curriculum for a specific subject, such as math or science. However, the company was able to gain additional market share and ranked first or second in 10 out of 11 adoption opportunities. It also guaranteed early leadership positions in Florida and Texas, through MH Plus platform, which witnessed 86% increase in educational districts. The impact of this growth should appear by 2027.

Operating Leverage & Cash Margins

The gross margin reached 85.3%, with 1% increase compared to the same period last year. This makes sense as the cost of replicating and distributing digital products is almost zero. The adjusted EBITDA also rose by 7.7% to record $136 million, achieving a margin of 31.3%, which confirms the excellent operating leverage. Thus, any increase in revenues goes to net profit without being consumed by costs.

Guidance

The company targets revenues between $2.067 billion and $2.087 billion in 2026, along with adjusted EBITDA between $729 million and $739 million. This reflects the management's confidence in future contracts and is a result of gaining additional market share from competitors; it also aims to achieve an operating margin exceeding 37%, which confirms its shift towards the EdTech model.

Value Per Share

Since the company has a net debt of $2.17 billion and a market cap of approximately $2.57 billion, the EV is $4.74 billion. Management's EBITDA guidance for 2026 is around $734 million. Therefore, MH trades at a forward EV/EBITDA of approximately 6.45x. The company has two major players to compare with, which are Pearson (PSO) with a multiple of 7x and low single-digit growth in revenues. John Wiley (WLY) trades at 10x and significantly relies on research and scientific journals rather than educational tech. While McGraw-Hill grows at 24% in Higher Education with a gross margin of 85%, the market applies a discount on the stock due to its high debt of $2.68 billion and the legal risks. However, as the company continues to pay off debt regularly and obtain legal settlements, the market will reprice the stock. Assuming a multiple of 9x, the target EV will reach $6.6 billion. By deducting net debt, the equity value comes to $4.43 billion or $23 per share, which represents a potential upside of 70%.

Risks To Consider

In August 2025, the Attorney General of Florida filed a lawsuit against McGraw-Hill and its competitor Savvas Learning, as the state accuses the company of violating the most favored nation pricing requirement, which mandates publishers to offer Florida the lowest price they offer to any other county in the U.S. Governor DeSantis stated that the price difference ranges between $37.5 million and $60.5 million. In my opinion, this type of cases rarely goes to trial and will likely end with a settlement at a cost significantly lower than estimated and with payments made in installments. Even in the worst case, the company has a liquidity of 514 million in cash and 450 million in credit facilities, which would easily cover the penalty without facing a cash crisis or issuing new shares.

There is also another case filed by a group of authors and gained momentum in November 2024 when the appeal court reinstated it. The company was deducting the operating costs of its Connect platform before calculating the Authors' share which reduces authors' earnings by 25% to 35%. Therefore, if the court decided to force McGraw-Hill to change its method of calculating copyrights, this would slightly reduce the gross margin, which is a point we should monitor.

Also, a large portion of MH's cash flow is going toward paying interest on its debt. If any sudden recession occurs and affects the education budgets, this would increase the debt service burden.

Final Thoughts

The company is currently suffering from a series of short-term negative news, whether due to the lawsuits or the declining of K-12 segment. However, I believe that the expected return far outweighs the risks, which will pass over time, as the company has recurring revenues of 82% in addition to its smart shift to selling institutional solutions, with profit margins exceeding 85%. Therefore, I believe that once the company announces a settlement for its cases and begins to reap the rewards of 2027 sales, the market will re-rate the stock.

data.title: Consumer Sentiment Plunges To Lowest Level On Record
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Consumer sentiment plunged nearly 11% in April to its lowest level on record, a decline driven largely by the outbreak of the Iran conflict. The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index fell 5.7 points to 47.6 this month, missing the 51.6 forecast, as consumers explicitly cited geopolitical instability and rising gas prices for the unfavorable shift in the economy.

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index is a monthly survey of consumer confidence levels in the U.S. with regard to the economy, personal finances, business conditions, and buying conditions, conducted by the University of Michigan. There are two reports released each month - a preliminary report released mid-month and a final report released at the end of the month.

Joanne Hsu, the director of surveys, made the following comments:

Consumer sentiment sank about 11% this month, extending a decline that began with the start of the Iran conflict, and is currently about 9% below a year ago. Demographic groups across age, income, and political party all posted setbacks in sentiment, as did every component of the index, reflecting the widespread nature of this month’s fall. One-year expected business conditions plunged about 20% and is now 6% below last April. Assessments of personal finances declined about 11%, with consumers expressing a substantial increase in concerns over high prices and weaker asset values. Buying conditions for durables and vehicles worsened, again on the basis of high prices. Open ended comments show that many consumers blame the Iran conflict for unfavorable changes to the economy. Note that 98% of interviews were completed prior to the April 7th announcement of a temporary cease-fire. Economic expectations will likely improve after consumers gain confidence that the supply disruptions stemming from the Iran conflict have ended and gas prices have moderated.

A Historical Perspective on Recession Risk

The chart below provides a long-term perspective on this widely watched indicator. We have highlighted the index's value at the start of each recession. The current level of 47.6 is below the index's value at the start of all six recessions since its inception.

To put today’s report in historical context, consumer sentiment is currently 43.3% below its average reading of 83.9 (arithmetic mean) and 42.4% below its geometric mean of 82.7, based on data dating back to 1978.

To visualize the volatility and its impact on the broader economy, the following chart includes a three-month moving average and real GDP. Historically, prolonged periods where the moving average remains at these depressed levels have closely correlated with negative GDP growth (the red bars below).

The Political and Presidential Lens

Each month, the survey results highlight sentiment within each political party. Sentiment is often viewed through a partisan lens, but the data shows that sentiment has fluctuated both positively and negatively under both Republican and Democratic administrations. As the chart below illustrates, the current "plunge" is a rare moment of bipartisan agreement, with declines seen across the political spectrum as energy costs hit every household.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Components

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index consists of two sub-indexes: the Current Economic Conditions Index (CECI) and the Consumer Expectations Index (CEI). The CECI reflects consumers' views of their current financial situation and the overall economy, while the CEI gauges their outlook for the future.

While both measures fell in April, a concerning gap is opening between how consumers feel today and what they fear for tomorrow.

  • Current Economic Conditions: Fell for a second straight month to 50.1, its lowest level on record. This represents a 10.2% decline from the previous month and a 16.2% drop from a year ago.
  • Consumer Expectations: Fell for a third straight month to 46.1, its lowest level since 1980. This represents a 10.8% decline from the previous month and a 2.5% drop from one year ago.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Inflation Expectations

Year-ahead inflation expectations surged from 3.8% in March to 4.8% this month, the largest one-month increase since April 2025 (see chart, black dashed line and black circle). The current reading exceeds those seen in 2024 and remains well above the 2.3-3.0% range seen in the two years pre-pandemic. Long-run inflation expectations ticked up from 3.2% last month to 3.4% this month, the highest reading since November 2025. In 2024, values ranged between 2.8% and 3.2%, while in 2019 and 2020, they were consistently below 2.8%.

Other Sentiment Indicators

For an additional perspective on consumer attitudes, see the most recent Conference Board's Consumer Confidence Index. Both indexes gauge consumer attitudes toward the current and future strength of the economy. However, the Consumer Confidence Index is more influenced by employment and labor market conditions, while the Michigan Sentiment Index is more focused on household finances and the impact of inflation.

The Conference Board index is the more volatile of the two, but the broad pattern and general trends have been remarkably similar to the Michigan index.

And finally, the prevailing mood of the Michigan survey is also similar to the mood of small business owners, as captured by the NFIB business optimism Index (monthly update here).

ETFs associated with sentiment include: Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
